Wear a certified helmet and eye protection on every ride.
Perform a pre-ride check of tires, brakes, lights, and fluid levels.
Use both brakes progressively for stable stopping.
Avoid abrupt braking while leaned over in a curve.
Look through the curve toward your exit point.
Reduce speed before entering curves and accelerate smoothly out.
Choose lane position to maximize visibility and escape space.
Do not ride for long periods in vehicle blind spots.
Signal early before every turn or lane change.
Use mirrors and a head check before lateral movement.
At intersections, assume other drivers may not see you.
Increase following distance in rain or low-traction conditions.
Use smooth controls on wet paint, metal, and slick surfaces.
Cross railroad tracks at a safe angle and controlled speed.
On gravel, reduce lean angle and use gentle control inputs.
Apply throttle progressively to avoid upsetting balance.
Wear high-visibility gear to improve conspicuity.
In crosswinds, reduce speed and hold a stable line.
Do not ride fatigued; fatigue slows reaction and judgment.
Never ride under alcohol, drugs, or impairing medication.
With a passenger, increase braking distance and smooth all inputs.
Adjust tire pressure and suspension for added load.
Secure cargo firmly and keep weight balanced.
Keep feet on pegs and hands on controls while moving.
Do not lane-split where it is illegal or unsafe.
Use daytime lights to improve front visibility.
If a tire loses pressure, roll off throttle and brake gradually.
Practice emergency stops and swerves in a safe area.
Do not fixate on hazards; look toward your escape path.
When entering freeways, match speed before merging.
When being passed, hold line and preserve side space.
Do not share one lane with another vehicle without legal safe room.
Before passing, confirm visibility, distance, and safe return path.
On wet roads, apply front brake with extra smoothness.
Maintain chain or final drive per manufacturer schedule.
Confirm throttle snaps closed when released.
Verify brake light activates from both brake controls.
Keep mirrors clean and correctly adjusted before riding.
Do not ride unfamiliar motorcycles without controlled practice first.
In city traffic, preserve escape space on both sides.
At stops, position where you remain visible to drivers ahead.
Avoid following large vehicles that block your view.
On long descents, combine engine braking and service brakes.
Avoid abrupt rear-wheel lock from excessive brake input.
If ABS engages, keep firm pressure and let it work.
At night, ride at a speed that fits your headlight range.
Keep visor clean to reduce glare and improve contrast.
Do not tailgate; motorcycles need reaction and braking margin.
Before moving, confirm side stand is up and transmission is ready.
Before turning, slow to an appropriate entry speed.
Do not enter blind curves fast; prioritize visibility and control.
In dense traffic, increase scanning and avoid trapped positions.
Always obey speed limits and road signs.
Use Search-Evaluate-Execute to manage risk continuously.
In heavy rain, avoid deep puddles and oily lane centers.
Do not brake or accelerate hard on wet metal plates.
Ride with safety margin for errors by other drivers.
Position where you can be seen in others’ mirrors.
Keep protective gear on until fully stopped in a safe area.
Plan route, fuel stops, and rest breaks before long rides.
Ride within your skill level and current road conditions.
